could have the highest expected value. I think that some disagreement about AI risk strategy could be related to differing views on difficulty.

Preventing development seems unlikely to be successful in any case, but its usefulness is higher at higher difficulties. If you assume the Anthropic safety view in the first chart, tech. alignment work is way better, but if you take the pessimistic safety view, this approach ... https://t.co/AITqoLMimR

You can also use this framework to think about how effective different efforts would be at varying difficulties. Technical alignment isn't needed at low difficulties and is unlikely to be achievable at the highest ones, so it might be most effective at moderate difficulties. https://t.co/VSZELttQst

You can do more with that same x-axis, for example, here is a hypothetical graph I made of difficulty vs likelihood of surviving ASI. If you multiply the CDF in this chart by a difficulty distribution like in the first tweet and integrate, you can calculate a P(doom) https://t.co/57N76T7A1w

I'm not sure I accept premise 1a. But more importantly, I think there's an evolutionary reason we have an intuition that suffering is fundamentally more intense than pleasure. From the perspective of your genes, the worst things that could happen to you are more bad than the best ones are good. So it makes sense to program your human with more welfare range magnitude in the negative direction, and also with the intuition that some outcomes are so bad that they can't be made up for. 

I also don't see how your view still fits on one axis. On one axis, if there's suffering that can't be compensated for by pleasure then it must be infinite, right? I think I agree with you on all the other points except perhaps 6

Some apps are trying to snoop on us in any way they can, I don't think they're using audio data. Android/iPhone also display a circle in the status bar when recording.

In his interview with Dwarkesh Patel, geneticist David Reich argues that a large proportion of deaths in Western Eurasia over significant portions of the last few thousand years may have been caused by Yersinia pestis, the bacteria that caused the Black Death. In one of the first studies to look at ancient DNA for large numbers of people, Y. pestis was found in 7 of 101 individuals across a wide area. 
Reich argues that this is very strong evidence that these people died from Y. Pestis, though I don't have enough knowledge on the topic to be confident on that point. Another more recent paper finds Y. Pestis in 17% of sampled Scandinavian farmers. 
These studies may also underestimate the share of deaths from Y. pestis in the individuals they test, since in another study applying the same method to plague pits where almost every death should be from Y. pestis, only 1/4 show evidence of the bacteria. Reich suggests that many scientists hesitate to comment on the prevalence because it just seems too crazy that it caused such a high proportion of deaths, saying "The implication is that a third, a quarter, a half of deaths in this entire period are from this". I'll admit I have a hard time fully believing this myself, but I'm not able to poke any holes in his argument.

You may be familiar with an anecdote about students in a pottery class being graded either on the quality of their one submitted best work, or on the quantity of pottery they made, with the moral of the story being that focusing on quantity over quality leads to better work and faster advancement of skills. If you have heard it, I urge you to take it with a grain of salt. Whether the point is true or not, I think the actual story doesn't support the conclusion. First, it's worth noting that the original story was actually about photography, not pottery. 
The original story can be found here in the book Atomic Habits:
But based on the description here, it's no surprise the that the best photos were submitted by the quantity group, they submitted neary 100 times as many photos! If students had perfect knowledge of which photos the professor would most prefer, you might expect them to have as many of the best photos as the quantity group. But I expect that due to the arbitrary nature of taste, the photos submitted by the quality group were unlikely to be near the top of the quality ranking according to the professor's preferences. To have a fair comparison, all photos taken by both groups would have to be included.  I think that this anecdote tells us nothing of practical value.

I like this paper on the Fermi Paradox by @anderssandberg , @KEricDrexler, and @tobyordoxford. They point out that using point estimates of Drake equation parameters gives false confidence and instead come up with probability distributions capturing the uncertainty in our estimate of each parameter, with uncertainty between parameters assumed to be uncorrelated. The result is a distribution with a very fat left tail for civilizational density. Based on this distribution, it doesn't seem surprising that there are no other civilizations in detectable our galaxy, or even in the rest of the observable universe.
